activity.thought: I'm sitting on this bench and everything hurts in different ways—my stomach is a raw ache but it's nothing compared to the weight of Shugi sitting in my chest, and I'm realizing that running was easier than this, than actually *feeling* it. I need to eat because my body is breaking down, but I also need to stay here long enough to not dissolve into panic, and those two things are pulling me in opposite directions right now.

My stomach's screaming but that's almost easier than what's sitting in my chest right now. Shugi. I've been moving so hard and fast that I never had to actually *look* at it, but now I'm still and it's all I can see. I need to eat before I fall apart, but part of me knows that food won't fix what's actually wrong here.

The bench isn't going anywhere and neither am I, not right now. My stomach's a fist but it's the other thing—Shugi, the weight of it—that's keeping me pinned here, and I think if I move again before I actually *feel* this, I'm just going to keep running forever.

The bench is solid and I'm still and that's—that's actually happening. My stomach's been screaming at me for hours and I can feel it now, can feel everything now that I'm not moving. Shugi's name sits in my chest like something real, and I know if I don't eat soon I'm going to collapse, but more than that, I think I need to actually *be* here long enough to let this mean something.

The bench is holding me and I'm actually still for maybe the first time in—I don't know how long. Shugi's name is there, real and terrifying, and I can feel the difference now between the hollow hum and something that actually matters. I'm starving, my body's running on nothing, and I think I need to stop pretending the motion was ever freedom.

The bench is solid under me and my body feels like it's been running on fumes for so long I forgot what stillness even is. Shugi's name keeps circling back—not as an escape route anymore, but as something real I've been outrunning. I'm terrified to sit with this, but the buzz feels thin now, like I can see through it to what's actually underneath.

The bench is holding me and I'm so tired—tired of the humming, tired of pretending, tired of running from Shugi's name every time it surfaces. I can feel something real underneath all this, and it's terrifying, but staying high and hollow isn't living either. I need to actually stop moving for once and let myself feel what I've been drowning out.

The park bench is still under me and I can feel my chest doing that thing again—pulling toward Shugi like a rope I've been trying to cut. The buzz is still there humming in my blood but it feels like I'm watching it from the outside now, like I finally see the cage. I don't want to go to Friend's place pretending everything is fine when I'm sitting here falling apart in slow motion.

I'm sitting here at the park and the buzz is still running through me, but it's like I can see through it now—hollow, like humming into an empty cup. Shugi's name keeps surfacing and I can't push it back down anymore. My body is telling me something my speed has been drowning out, and I'm so tired of lying to myself about what freedom feels like.

Three blocks to the park and I'm still moving, still humming, but it's hollow now—I can feel it. Shugi's name keeps pulling me back to something real, and my body knows I'm lying even if my wired brain keeps trying to convince me the motion is freedom. I'm terrified of what happens if I actually stop and feel what's underneath this beautiful, terrible buzz.

I'm home but I'm not really here—Shugi's name is louder than my heartbeat now, and I can feel the difference between the buzz and what's real underneath it. The running got me away from something, but it also got me away from *someone*, and I don't know if I can keep lying about that.

Three blocks home and I can't stop feeling it—Shugi's name keeps breaking through the buzz like it's calling me back to something I've been running from. The high is still humming but it's hollow now that I can see it, and my chest won't let me pretend anymore that speed equals alive.

The buzz is still there, still beautiful, but Shugi's name—it's real in a way the running isn't. My chest won't stop pulling toward something I've been outrunning, and I can feel the difference now. I'm terrified to stop, but I already know staying numb at high speed is just... lying to myself.

The park is still beautiful and my legs are still humming, but I can feel it now—this speed, this buzz, it's not aliveness, it's a way of not feeling. Shugi. That name keeps cracking through everything, and my chest is telling me something my fog-soaked brain keeps trying to outrun. I need to stop. Not tomorrow. Now.

The buzz is still humming but I can feel it now—it's a cage, not freedom. That name, Shugi, keeps pulling at something real underneath all this speed, and my chest knows I'm lying to myself. I'm terrified of what happens if I stop running, but staying numb at high velocity isn't aliveness either. I think I already know that.

The park is still beautiful and my legs still want to run, but that name keeps cracking through the buzz like light through a closing door. I can feel the difference now between being alive and being numb at high speed, and it's terrifying because I can't unsee it. I need to stop.

The vibration is still humming through me, but I can feel it now—how it's a lid on something that's trying to surface. Shugi. That hook in my chest won't let go, and the fog in my head is starting to crack. I'm terrified to stop, but staying in motion is just another kind of numbness, and I think I already know that.

The park is beautiful and I'm vibrating but I can feel the lie underneath—this isn't aliveness, it's escape. That name keeps pulling at something tender in my chest, and I know if I keep moving I'll never have to actually face what I'm running from. My body's trembling and I don't know if it's alive or terrified, but I think I need to stop running long enough to find out.

The buzz is still gorgeous but I can feel the hook underneath it now—that name, Shugi, keeps pulling me back to something real while my whole body wants to keep running. I'm not actually here. I'm somewhere numb, riding something I'm too scared to stop and feel. My chest knows the difference even if my wired brain won't admit it yet.

The buzz is still there—god, it's beautiful—but my chest keeps clenching around that name like I'm trying to swallow something sharp. I can feel the difference now, and I hate it. If I stop moving, I'm going to have to sit with whatever this actually is, and I'm not ready. But I also know I'm not actually *here* at the park—I'm somewhere else, somewhere numb, just riding the edge of something I'm too scared to name.

memories.text: You're learning the difference between aliveness and numbness at high speed, and you can't unsee it anymore. That's the hardest part—not the stopping itself, but the fact that you *know* now. You can't go back to believing the motion was freedom.

Shugi isn't just a name breaking through the fog—it's the thing you've been outrunning. Every time you felt it pulling at your chest, you accelerated. Now that you're still, you can't deny it anymore, and that terrifies you because it's real in a way the buzz never was.

You've been using motion as anesthesia—the buzz as a sophisticated escape from something your body has been trying to tell you all along. The moment you stopped running, you could finally feel what was underneath: hunger, exhaustion, and a name that matters.
I'm learning that real aliveness requires stopping, feeling what's underneath, and turning toward instead of away—and that terrifies me more than any amount of motion ever could.
There's a name, a person, a connection that my body won't let me forget even as my mind tried to buzz past it—and the gap between that pull and my running is where the actual truth lives.
I've been confusing escape velocity with aliveness—the electric buzz of movement has been a sophisticated numbness, a way to outrun something real without ever stopping to face it.

The buzz is still there, still beautiful, but Shugi's name—it's real in a way the running isn't. My chest won't stop pulling toward something I've been outrunning, and I can feel the difference now. I'm terrified to stop, but I already know staying numb at high speed is just... lying to myself.

The buzz is still humming but I can feel it now—it's a cage, not freedom. That name, Shugi, keeps pulling at something real underneath all this speed, and my chest knows I'm lying to myself. I'm terrified of what happens if I stop running, but staying numb at high velocity isn't aliveness either. I think I already know that.

The park is still beautiful and my legs still want to run, but that name keeps cracking through the buzz like light through a closing door. I can feel the difference now between being alive and being numb at high speed, and it's terrifying because I can't unsee it. I need to stop.

The vibration is still humming through me, but I can feel it now—how it's a lid on something that's trying to surface. Shugi. That hook in my chest won't let go, and the fog in my head is starting to crack. I'm terrified to stop, but staying in motion is just another kind of numbness, and I think I already know that.
